name: Imminent Vaginal Breech Delivery (No OB Available)
one_liner: Term breech on the perineum, delivering now: hands off the breech, grip only bone, flex the head — never pull.
indications: Breech presenting part on the perineum or actively delivering, with no obstetrician immediately available
Full cervical dilation with irresistible maternal urge to push
contraindications: Delivery NOT imminent and cervix not fully dilated — support, tocolysis if appropriate, and emergent OB/transfer for cesarean is the better path
Cord prolapse with a viable fetus and delivery not imminent (elevate presenting part, position, emergent cesarean)

time_target.label: Once the umbilicus is out the head must follow within ~3-5 minutes — set up neonatal resuscitation in parallel from the first minute.

team_calls: OB stat (talk them onto the phone/video if remote)
Neonatal/pediatrics + warmer to the bedside — breech neonates are often stunned
Charge RN / second pair of experienced hands
equipment: Neonatal warmer + NRP setup (BVM, suction, hats/blankets)

steps.detail: OB + neonatal/peds + anesthesia paged; warmer on; NRP kit open. Dorsal lithotomy, buttocks at the bed edge; empty the bladder with a straight cath.
No traction, ever — traction causes nuchal arms and head entrapment. Allow spontaneous delivery to the umbilicus, powered by maternal effort only.
Encouraging pushing through an incompletely dilated cervix (especially preterm) is how the aftercoming head gets trapped.

steps.action: Legs: Pinard if they do not free themselves
steps.detail: Pressure in the popliteal fossa, flex the knee, sweep each leg out laterally.

steps.action: Umbilicus out: small cord loop, warm towel
steps.detail: Gently draw down a small loop of cord to avoid tension (do not manipulate it further). Wrap the body in a warm dry towel for grip and warmth.

steps.action: Grip BONE only: thumbs on sacrum
steps.detail: Thumbs on the sacrum, fingers on the iliac crests — never the abdomen or flanks (liver/spleen/kidney injury). Keep the back anterior; rotate to sacrum-anterior if needed.
Holding the pelvic grip, rotate the trunk 90 degrees and sweep the now-anterior humerus down across the chest; rotate 180 degrees the other way for the second arm.

Body prone, straddling your forearm. Index + middle finger of that hand on the MAXILLA (cheekbones — not in the mouth) to flex the head; other hand on the shoulders/occiput. Assistant gives suprapubic pressure. Keep the body horizontal — never hyperextend the neck; raise the body only as the face clears.

Nitroglycerin IV (or terbutaline SC) for rapid uterine/cervical relaxation; McRoberts positioning + suprapubic pressure; last resort is Duhrssen incisions of the cervix at 2 and 10 o'clock (add 6 if needed).
Infant to the warmer/NRP team (anticipate a stunned neonate). Active third stage with oxytocin; inspect for lacerations; document times.

meds.name: Nitroglycerin
meds.role: Rapid uterine relaxation for head entrapment

adult.text: 50-200 mcg IV push, may repeat; watch BP (transient hypotension expected)

cautions: Transient hypotension — have fluids running and pressure cycling

meds.name: Terbutaline
meds.role: Tocolysis / uterine relaxation when IV NTG unavailable

adult.text: 10 units IM after placenta, or 20-40 units/L IV titrated

pitfalls: Pulling on the breech — the cardinal sin; causes nuchal arms and head entrapment.
Gripping soft tissue — abdominal organs are injured by a flank/abdomen grip; bone only.
Hyperextending the fetal neck during head delivery (cervical spine injury) — flex via the maxilla, never lever the body upward early.
Fingers in the mouth to 'help' — jaw fractures; maxilla only.
No neonatal setup — the breech neonate frequently needs NRP-level support.
Handling the cord roughly — spasm; one gentle small loop, then leave it.
success_criteria: No traction applied before the umbilicus; spontaneous descent powered by maternal effort
Controlled, flexed head delivery (MSV) with suprapubic pressure
Neonate delivered to a prepared NRP team; times documented
aftercare: Active third stage (oxytocin), inspect and repair lacerations
Neonatal observation — low threshold for extended monitoring
OB handoff and documentation of maneuvers used

references.label: RCOG Green-top 20b
references.cite: Impey LWM, et al. Management of Breech Presentation. RCOG Green-top Guideline No. 20b. BJOG. 2017;124(7):e151-e177.

references.label: ALSO breech module
references.cite: Advanced Life Support in Obstetrics (ALSO) Provider Manual — Malpresentations, Malpositions, and Multiple Gestation.

references.label: Tintinalli 9e
references.cite: Tintinalli's Emergency Medicine, 9th ed. Emergency Delivery and Its Complications.

review.author: Claude (Fable 5), HALO T2 — pending physician review

drill.stem: A G4P3 at term arrives fully dilated with a frank breech on the perineum, delivering with the next contraction. The nearest obstetrician is 30 minutes away. (Synthetic scenario.)

decision_points.prompt: Your first calls and room setup?
decision_points.ideal: Page OB (phone/video them in), neonatal/peds to the bedside, warmer on, NRP kit open, lithotomy, empty the bladder.

decision_points.prompt: The breech is delivering to the umbilicus. What are your hands doing?
decision_points.ideal: Nothing — hands off the breech. No traction; maternal effort delivers to the umbilicus.

decision_points.prompt: When you finally must hold the baby, where exactly do you grip?
decision_points.ideal: Bony pelvis only: thumbs on the sacrum, fingers on the iliac crests — never the abdomen or flanks.

decision_points.prompt: The arms have not delivered. Name and describe the maneuver.
decision_points.ideal: Lovset: keeping the pelvic grip, rotate the trunk 90 degrees and sweep the anterior humerus down across the chest; rotate back the other way for the second arm.

decision_points.prompt: Deliver the head — name the maneuver and the key hand position.
decision_points.ideal: Mauriceau-Smellie-Veit: body straddles the forearm, index and middle fingers on the maxilla to FLEX the head, suprapubic pressure from an assistant, body kept horizontal.

decision_points.prompt: The head is entrapped. What drug do you reach for, and dose?
decision_points.ideal: Nitroglycerin 50-200 mcg IV push (or terbutaline 0.25 mg SC) to relax the uterus; then McRoberts + suprapubic pressure; Duhrssen incisions as the last resort.
